steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks how the author of Text 1 would respond to Text 2's claim that Silva and colleagues' approach ‘could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.’ Text 1's central argument is that genre categories are ‘ambiguous, subjective, and simplistic’ and that music may be best understood through frameworks other than genre. The author is skeptical that genre classification is useful at all — so even a technically improved classifier would not address the underlying problem Text 1 identifies.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "By suggesting that future research may provide substantial advancements in the field of automated genre classification": Text 1 criticizes the usefulness of genre classification itself, not its technical accuracy. Praising future advancements would contradict the author's position. ✗
  • "By asserting that genre classifications may not be the most helpful way to think about music": This directly restates Text 1's core claim. The author would redirect Text 2's focus: even if classification improves, it may not be useful because genre is the wrong framework. ✓
  • "By emphasizing that humans do not necessarily enjoy every recording in a genre they claim to prefer": Text 1 never discusses listener enjoyment or preference patterns. This introduces an idea not present in the passage. ✗
  • "By arguing that some genres are more easily recognized by classification systems than others": This would be a technical point about classifier performance — the kind of argument Text 1 implicitly dismisses as beside the point. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
